header: Gerente de People & Culture — Maple Bear
hub_subtitle: Business English para People & Culture, comunicação executiva e a liderança da divisão do Canadá

lesson.menu_title: Brazil vs. Canada -- Articulating Cultural Differences with Academic and Executive Teams
lesson.menu_desc: Cleft sentences, hedging e o vocabulário que compara duas culturas sem generalizar nenhuma -- 34 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 5 -- Brazil vs. Canada
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Danielle Moreira | Aula 5 -- Brazil vs. Canada

listenings.text: Danielle, it is Claire. I have read your cultural brief, and I want to talk about one sentence in it before anybody else does. Page two. Brazilians avoid conflict. That is the sentence. No hedge, no evidence, and a claim about two hundred million people. Here is what will happen if the faculty reads that. They will not argue with you. They will simply stop reading, and everything true in the other nine pages will go down with it. Now, the second thing, and this one matters more. You wrote that our last engagement session went well because nobody objected. Danielle, nobody objects in that room. Two of those professors have been running their faculties for twenty years, and they say no the way they have always said no: in writing, three days later, in three very careful paragraphs. So do not read that silence as agreement. What I want you to bring on Thursday is not a comparison of two countries. Bring me one observation with a number behind it, and bring the words that say it is a tendency and not a rule. That is the difference between a diagnosis they will fight with you about, and a document they will quietly put in a drawer.

Danielle, this is Marc. A note before tomorrow, and I would rather you hear it from me than learn it in the room. There is one sentence that will end that session early. It begins with, in Brazil we, and it ends with a rule. Say that, and this faculty will hear a stereotype and stop listening to your evidence, and I will not be able to get them back. That is not hostility. It is how doctors are trained: we attack the claim, not the person, and a claim without a hedge is simply a bad claim. The second thing you should know is that nobody will contradict you tomorrow. Not one person. And you will leave the room believing it went well. Then the disagreement will arrive on Friday, in an email, in three careful paragraphs, and it will be copied to your director. That is how we do it here, and I am telling you because nobody else will. So here is what I actually want. Set out the expectations on the first day, in writing, so that we are not guessing in November. And then ask me the question nobody in this organization has ever asked me. Not what I need. What I think the problem is.

items.situation: "You write that our teams are harder to read. Are you not at risk of overgeneralizing?"
tips: I would be, if I were describing people. I am describing tendencies, and more often than not, difficult feedback in our Brazilian teams is delivered indirectly.
hedge the claim -- never withdraw it

items.situation: "Nobody objected in that session. How can you call it a failure?"
tips: It was the power distance in that room that produced the silence, not agreement. Any diagnosis that does not account for hierarchy will read silence as consent.
the it-cleft: choose the cause, and rule the other one out

items.situation: "So what exactly is this faculty getting? Another framework?"
tips: What this faculty needs is not another framework. It is to be asked before it is told.
the wh-cleft: hold the room, then land it

items.situation: "How is leading People and Culture here going to be different from Brazil?"
tips: For the most part, our Canadian colleagues expect the expectations to be set out in writing, and what surprised me was how much of the disagreement arrives three days later.
one dimension, one hedge, one observation

items.situation: "You are bringing a Brazilian model and translating it into English."
tips: I am not arriving with a model to install. What I am bringing is a diagnosis, and it begins with cultural humility rather than a template that worked somewhere else.
cultural humility is a strategy, not an apology

items.situation: "Which culture is better at this, honestly?"
tips: That said, I would be careful not to overgeneralize. There is more variation inside each country than between them, and my job is to bridge cultural gaps rather than rank them.
never rank two cultures in front of either one
